1	#pragma once
2	/**
3	 * @file
4	 * @author  Alex Singer
5	 * @date    October 2024
6	 * @brief   The declarations of the Global Placer base class which is used to
7	 *          define the functionality of all global placers in the AP flow.
8	 *
9	 * A Global Placer creates a Partial Placement given only the netlist and the
10	 * architecture. It uses analytical techniques (i.e. efficient numerical
11	 * minimization of an objective function of a placement) to find a placement
12	 * that optimizes for objectives subject to some of the constraints of the FPGA
13	 * architecture.
14	 */
15	
16	#include <memory>
17	#include "ap_flow_enums.h"
18	#include "flat_placement_density_manager.h"
19	#include "partial_legalizer.h"
20	
21	// Forward declarations
22	class APNetlist;
23	class AnalyticalSolver;
24	class LogicalModels;
25	class PartialLegalizer;
26	class PlaceDelayModel;
27	class PreClusterTimingManager;
28	class Prepacker;
29	struct PartialPlacement;
30	
31	/**
32	 * @brief The Global Placer base class
33	 *
34	 * This declares the functionality that all Global Placers will use. This
35	 * provides a standard interface for the global placers so they can be used
36	 * interchangeably. This makes it very easy to test and compare different global
37	 * placers.
38	 */
39	class GlobalPlacer {
40	  public:
41	    virtual ~GlobalPlacer() {}
42	
43	    /**
44	     * @brief Constructor of the base GlobalPlacer class
45	     *
46	     *  @param netlist      Netlist of the design at some abstraction level;
47	     *                      typically this would have some atoms and groups of
48	     *                      atoms (in a pack pattern).
49	     *  @param log_verbosity    The verbosity of log messages in the Global
50	     *                          Placer.
51	     */
52	    GlobalPlacer(const APNetlist& ap_netlist, int log_verbosity)
53	        : ap_netlist_(ap_netlist)
54	        , log_verbosity_(log_verbosity) {}
55	
56	    /**
57	     * @brief Perform global placement on the given netlist.
58	     *
59	     * The role of a global placer is to try and find a placement for the given
60	     * netlist which optimizes some objective function and is mostly legal.
61	     */
62	    virtual PartialPlacement place() = 0;
63	
64	  protected:
65	    /// @brief The APNetlist the global placer is placing.
66	    const APNetlist& ap_netlist_;
67	
68	    /// @brief The setting of how verbose the log messages should be in the
69	    ///        global placer. Anything larger than zero will display per
70	    ///        iteration status messages.
71	    int log_verbosity_;
72	};
73	
74	/**
75	 * @brief A factory method which creates a Global Placer of the given type.
76	 */
77	std::unique_ptr<GlobalPlacer> make_global_placer(e_ap_analytical_solver analytical_solver_type,
78	                                                 e_ap_partial_legalizer partial_legalizer_type,
79	                                                 const APNetlist& ap_netlist,
80	                                                 const Prepacker& prepacker,
81	                                                 const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
82	                                                 const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
83	                                                 const std::vector<t_logical_block_type>& logical_block_types,
84	                                                 const std::vector<t_physical_tile_type>& physical_tile_types,
85	                                                 const LogicalModels& models,
86	                                                 PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
87	                                                 std::shared_ptr<PlaceDelayModel> place_delay_model,
88	                                                 float ap_timing_tradeoff,
89	                                                 bool generate_mass_report,
90	                                                 const std::vector<std::string>& target_density_arg_strs,
91	                                                 unsigned num_threads,
92	                                                 int log_verbosity);
93	
94	/**
95	 * @brief A Global Placer based on the SimPL work for analytical ASIC placement.
96	 *          https://doi.org/10.1145/2461256.2461279
97	 *
98	 * This placement technique uses a solver to generate a placement that optimizes
99	 * over some objective function and is likely very illegal (has many overlapping
100	 * blocks and blocks in the wrong places). This solution represents the "lower-
101	 * bound" on the solution quality.
102	 *
103	 * This technique passes this "lower-bound" solution into a legalizer, which
104	 * tries to find the closest legal solution to the lower-bound solution (by
105	 * spreading out blocks and placing them in legal positions). This often
106	 * destroys the quality of the lower-bound solution, and is considered an
107	 * "upper-bound" on the solution quality.
108	 *
109	 * Each iteration of this global placer, the upper-bound solution is fed into
110	 * the solver as a "hint" to what a legal solution looks like. This allows the
111	 * solver to produce another placement which will make decisions knowing where
112	 * the blocks will end-up in the legal solution. This worstens the quality of
113	 * the lower-bound solution; however, after passing this solution back into
114	 * the legalizer, this will likely improve the quality of the upper-bound
115	 * solution.
116	 *
117	 * Over several iterations the upper-bound and lower-bound solutions will
118	 * approach each other until a good quality, mostly-legal solution is found.
119	 */
120	class SimPLGlobalPlacer : public GlobalPlacer {
121	  private:
122	    /// @brief The maximum number of iterations the global placer can perform.
123	    static constexpr size_t max_num_iterations_ = 100;
124	
125	    /// @brief The target relative gap between the HPWL of the upper-bound and
126	    ///        lower-bound placements. The placer will stop if the difference
127	    ///        between the two bounds, normalized to the upper-bound, is smaller
128	    ///        than this number.
129	    ///        This number was empircally found to work well.
130	    static constexpr double target_hpwl_relative_gap_ = 0.01;
131	
132	    /// @brief The solver which generates the lower-bound placement.
133	    std::unique_ptr<AnalyticalSolver> solver_;
134	
135	    /// @brief The density manager the partial legalizer will optimize over.
136	    std::shared_ptr<FlatPlacementDensityManager> density_manager_;
137	
138	    /// @brief The legalizer which generates the upper-bound placement.
139	    std::unique_ptr<PartialLegalizer> partial_legalizer_;
140	
141	    /// @brief The pre-cluster timing manager which manages how the timing of
142	    ///        the netlist is computed.
143	    PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager_;
144	
145	    /// @brief A placement delay model which is used to help compute the delays
146	    ///        of connections in the AP netlist.
147	    std::shared_ptr<PlaceDelayModel> place_delay_model_;
148	
149	  public:
150	    /**
151	     * @brief Constructor for the SimPL Global Placer
152	     *
153	     * Constructs the solver and partial legalizer.
154	     */
155	    SimPLGlobalPlacer(e_ap_analytical_solver analytical_solver_type,
156	                      e_ap_partial_legalizer partial_legalizer_type,
157	                      const APNetlist& ap_netlist,
158	                      const Prepacker& prepacker,
159	                      const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
160	                      const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
161	                      const std::vector<t_logical_block_type>& logical_block_types,
162	                      const std::vector<t_physical_tile_type>& physical_tile_types,
163	                      const LogicalModels& models,
164	                      PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
165	                      std::shared_ptr<PlaceDelayModel> place_delay_model,
166	                      float ap_timing_tradeoff,
167	                      bool generate_mass_report,
168	                      const std::vector<std::string>& target_density_arg_strs,
169	                      unsigned num_threads,
170	                      int log_verbosity);
171	
172	    /**
173	     * @brief Run a SimPL-like global placement algorithm
174	     *
175	     * This iteratively runs the solver and legalizer until a good quality and
176	     * mostly-legal placement is found.
177	     */
178	    PartialPlacement place() final;
179	};

2	/**
3	 * @file
4	 * @author  Alex Singer
5	 * @date    September 2024
6	 * @brief   Defines the FullLegalizer class which takes a partial AP placement
7	 *          and generates a fully legal clustering and placement which can be
8	 *          routed by VTR.
9	 */
10	
11	#include <memory>
12	#include <unordered_set>
13	#include "ap_flow_enums.h"
14	#include "cluster_legalizer.h"
15	
16	// Forward declarations
17	class APNetlist;
18	class AtomNetlist;
19	class ClusteredNetlist;
20	class DeviceGrid;
21	struct PartialPlacement;
22	class PlaceMacros;
23	class PreClusterTimingManager;
24	class Prepacker;
25	class RamMapper;
26	struct t_arch;
27	struct t_vpr_setup;

29	/**
30	 * @brief The full legalizer in an AP flow
31	 *
32	 * Given a valid partial placement (of any level of legality), will produce a
33	 * fully legal clustering and clustered placement for use in the rest of the
34	 * VTR flow.
35	 */
36	class FullLegalizer {
37	  public:
38	    virtual ~FullLegalizer() {}
39	
40	    FullLegalizer(const APNetlist& ap_netlist,
41	                  const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
42	                  const Prepacker& prepacker,
43	                  const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
44	                  const RamMapper& ram_mapper,
45	                  const t_vpr_setup& vpr_setup,
46	                  const t_arch& arch,
47	                  const DeviceGrid& device_grid)
48	        : ap_netlist_(ap_netlist)
49	        , atom_netlist_(atom_netlist)
50	        , prepacker_(prepacker)
51	        , pre_cluster_timing_manager_(pre_cluster_timing_manager)
52	        , ram_mapper_(ram_mapper)
53	        , vpr_setup_(vpr_setup)
54	        , arch_(arch)
55	        , device_grid_(device_grid) {}
56	
57	    /**
58	     * @brief Perform legalization on the given partial placement solution
59	     *
60	     *  @param p_placement  A valid partial placement (passes verify method).
61	     *                      This implies that all blocks are placed on the
62	     *                      device grid and fixed blocks are observed.
63	     */
64	    virtual void legalize(const PartialPlacement& p_placement) = 0;
65	
66	    /// @brief Update drawing data structure for current placement
67	    void update_drawing_data_structures();
68	
69	  protected:
70	    /**
71	     * @brief Recreate the device grid (and RR graph if needed) after final
72	     *        clustering, when auto device sizing is used.
73	     *
74	     * The device grid estimated before packing may differ from the one needed
75	     * after final clustering. This method finalizes the device size and ensures
76	     * the RR graph matches it before placement begins.
77	     */
78	    void recreate_device_if_needed();
79	
80	    /// @brief The AP Netlist to fully legalize the flat placement of.
81	    const APNetlist& ap_netlist_;
82	
83	    /// @brief The Atom Netlist used to generate the AP Netlist.
84	    const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist_;
85	
86	    /// @brief The Prepacker used to create molecules from the Atom Netlist.
87	    const Prepacker& prepacker_;
88	
89	    /// @brief Pre-Clustering timing manager, hold pre-computed delay information
90	    ///        at the primitive level prior to packing.
91	    const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager_;
92	
93	    const RamMapper& ram_mapper_;
94	
95	    /// @brief The VPR setup options passed into the VPR flow. This must be
96	    ///        mutable since some parts of packing modify the options.
97	    const t_vpr_setup& vpr_setup_;
98	
99	    /// @brief Information on the architecture of the FPGA.
100	    const t_arch& arch_;
101	
102	    /// @brief The device grid which records where clusters can be placed.
103	    const DeviceGrid& device_grid_;
104	};

106	/**
107	 * @brief A factory method which creates a Full Legalizer of the given type.
108	 */
109	std::unique_ptr<FullLegalizer> make_full_legalizer(e_ap_full_legalizer full_legalizer_type,
110	                                                   const APNetlist& ap_netlist,
111	                                                   const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
112	                                                   const Prepacker& prepacker,
113	                                                   const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
114	                                                   const RamMapper& ram_mapper,
115	                                                   const t_vpr_setup& vpr_setup,
116	                                                   const t_arch& arch,
117	                                                   const DeviceGrid& device_grid);
118	
119	/**
120	 * @brief FlatRecon: The Flat Placement Reconstruction Full Legalizer.
121	 *
122	 * Reconstructs (packs and places) an input flat placement with minimal
123	 * disturbance. The flat placement may be read from a ``.fplace`` file or
124	 * taken from Global Placement (GP). In both cases the input is expected to be
125	 * near-legal.
126	 *
127	 * Before packing, molecules are sorted so that long carry chain molecules are
128	 * priorizited. For molecules in the same priority group, the number of external pins
129	 * is used as a tie-breaker. It then groups the molecules according to the tiles
130	 * determined from their flat placement.
131	 *
132	 * The packing consists of three passes:
133	 * 1) Self clustering: For each tile, form as few clusters as possible from
134	 *    molecules targeting that tile, and does not create more clusters than the
135	 *    tile can accommodate. Try clustering with the faster SKIP_INTRA_LB_ROUTE
136	 *    legality strategy (after each molecule is added) first; if the resulting
137	 *    cluster turns out to be unroutable, packing it is retried with the FULL
138	 *    legality checking strategy. Molecules that still cannot be clustered
139	 *    (incompatible with the tile or with the newly formed clusters) are passed
140	 *    to the neighbor pass.
141	 *
142	 * 2) Neighbor clustering: For each unclustered molecule, inspect clusters in
143	 *    the 8 neighboring tiles within the same layer. Tiles are processed in
144	 *    ascending order of average molecules-per-cluster. The unclustered molecule is
145	 *    added to an existing cluster if compatible (and all 8 are checked); no new
146	 *    clusters are created in this pass.
147	 *
148	 * 3) Orphan-window clustering: Remaining “orphan” molecules are clustered by
149	 *    repeated BFS expansions centered at seeds chosen by highest external input
150	 *    pin count. From each seed’s assigned location, try to cluster orphan molecules
151	 *    within a Manhattan distance ≤ radius. The default radius is 8 (empirically
152	 *    minimizes cluster count without inflating displacement on Titan benchmarks).
153	 *    If the resulting clusters do not fit the device, the pass is retried with
154	 *    radius 16, and finally with a radius spanning the whole device. Larger radii
155	 *    reduce cluster count but can increase displacement.
156	 *
157	 * After cluster creation, each cluster is placed by the initial placer at the
158	 * grid location nearest to the centroid of its atoms.
159	 */
160	class FlatRecon : public FullLegalizer {
161	  public:
162	    using FullLegalizer::FullLegalizer;
163	
164	    /**
165	     * @brief Perform the FlatRecon full legalization.
166	     */
167	    void legalize(const PartialPlacement& p_placement) final;
168	
169	  private:
170	    /// @brief Mapping from subtile location to legalization cluster id to keep
171	    ///        track of clusters created.
172	    /// TODO: It might make sense to store this as a 3D NDMatrix of arrays where we can
173	    ///       index into the [layer][x][y][subtile] and get the cluster ID at that location.
174	    ///       It will be faster than using an unordered map and likely more space efficient.
175	    std::unordered_map<t_pl_loc, LegalizationClusterId> loc_to_cluster_id_placed;
176	
177	    /// @brief Mapping from a molecule id to its desired physical tile location.
178	    vtr::vector<PackMoleculeId, t_physical_tile_loc> mol_desired_physical_tile_loc;
179	
180	    /// @brief Mapping from legalization cluster ids to subtile locations. Using
181	    ///        unordered_map instead of vtr::vector since LegalizationClusterIds
182	    ///        can have significant gaps as you create a new ID for each cluster
183	    ///        you attempt to create.
184	    std::unordered_map<LegalizationClusterId, t_pl_loc> cluster_locs;
185	
186	    /// @brief 3D NDMatrix of legalization cluster ids. Stores the cluster ids at
187	    ///        that tile location and can be accessed in the format of [layer][x][y].
188	    ///        This is stored to be used in the neighbor pass.
189	    vtr::NdMatrix<std::unordered_set<LegalizationClusterId>, 3> tile_clusters_matrix;
190	
191	    /**
192	     * @brief Helper method to sort and group molecules by desired tile location.
193	     *        It first sorts by being in a long carry chain, then by external input
194	     *        pin count.
195	     * @return Mapping from tile location to sorted vector of molecules that
196	     *         want to be in that tile.
197	     */
198	    std::unordered_map<t_physical_tile_loc, std::vector<PackMoleculeId>>
199	    sort_and_group_blocks_by_tile(const PartialPlacement& p_placement);
200	
201	    /**
202	     * @brief Helper method to create clusters at a given tile location using
203	     *        given vector of molecules.
204	     *
205	     * Iterates over each subtile in the same order each time, hence trying to
206	     * create the fewest clusters in that tile. It also checks the compatibility
207	     * of the molecules with the tile before creating a cluster. Stores the cluster
208	     * ids' to check their legality or clean afterwards if needed.
209	     *
210	     *  @param tile_loc                        The physical tile location that clusters aimed to be created.
211	     *  @param tile_type                       The physical type of the tile that clusters aimed to be created.
212	     *  @param tile_molecules                  A vector of molecule ids aimed to be placed in that tile.
213	     *  @param cluster_legalizer               The cluster legalizer which is used to create and grow clusters.
214	     *  @param primitive_candidate_block_types A list of candidate block types for the given molecule to create a cluster.
215	     *  @return The set of LegalizationClusterIds created in that tile.
216	     */
217	    std::unordered_set<LegalizationClusterId>
218	    cluster_molecules_in_tile(const t_physical_tile_loc& tile_loc,
219	                              const t_physical_tile_type_ptr& tile_type,
220	                              const std::vector<PackMoleculeId>& tile_molecules,
221	                              ClusterLegalizer& cluster_legalizer,
222	                              const vtr::vector<LogicalModelId, std::vector<t_logical_block_type_ptr>>& primitive_candidate_block_types);
223	
224	    /**
225	     * @brief Helper method to perform self clustering pass.
226	     *
227	     * Iterates over each tile and first tries to create the fewest clusters
228	     * in that tile with SKIP_INTRA_LB_ROUTE strategy. If the resulting
229	     * cluster is found to be unroutable when fully checked, retry adding the
230	     * molecules with the FULL strategy before going to next tile.
231	     *
232	     *  @param cluster_legalizer               The cluster legalizer which is used to create and grow clusters. The result of
233	     *                                         this pass is an updated cluster_legalizer.
234	     *  @param device_grid                     The device grid used to get physical tile types.
235	     *  @param primitive_candidate_block_types A list of candidate block types for the given molecule to create a cluster.
236	     *  @param tile_blocks                     The list of molecules to pack in each non-empty tile.
237	     */
238	    void self_clustering(ClusterLegalizer& cluster_legalizer,
239	                         const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
240	                         const vtr::vector<LogicalModelId, std::vector<t_logical_block_type_ptr>>& primitive_candidate_block_types,
241	                         std::unordered_map<t_physical_tile_loc, std::vector<PackMoleculeId>>& tile_blocks);
242	
243	    /**
244	     * @brief Helper method to perform neighbor clustering.
245	     *
246	     * For each unclustered molecule, examines clusters in the 8 neighboring tiles.
247	     * Neighbor tiles are processed in order of increasing average molecule density.
248	     * The molecule is then added to an existing cluster if compatible. No new
249	     * clusters are created in this pass.
250	     *
251	     *  @return The set of molecule ids clustered in that pass.
252	     */
253	    std::unordered_set<PackMoleculeId>
254	    neighbor_clustering(ClusterLegalizer& cluster_legalizer,
255	                        const vtr::vector<LogicalModelId, std::vector<t_logical_block_type_ptr>>& primitive_candidate_block_types);
256	
257	    /**
258	     * @brief Helper method to perform orphan window clustering.
259	     *
260	     * Iteratively selects a seed orphan molecule (highest external input pins).
261	     * From the seed’s assigned location, expands within the given Manhattan
262	     * search radius in a BFS manner to find nearby orphan molecules. Compatible
263	     * neighbors are added to the seed’s cluster in order of proximity. After
264	     * reaching the search radius, the process repeats with new seeds until all
265	     * orphan molecules are clustered.
266	     *
267	     *  @param cluster_legalizer               The cluster legalizer which is used to create and grow clusters.
268	     *  @param primitive_candidate_block_types A list of candidate block types for the given molecule to create a cluster.
269	     *  @param search_radius                   The search radius that determines the allowed max distance from the seed
270	     *                                         molecule to candidate molecules.
271	     *  @return The set of LegalizationClusterIds created in that pass.
272	     */
273	    std::unordered_set<LegalizationClusterId>
274	    orphan_window_clustering(ClusterLegalizer& cluster_legalizer,
275	                             const vtr::vector<LogicalModelId, std::vector<t_logical_block_type_ptr>>& primitive_candidate_block_types,
276	                             int search_radius);
277	    /**
278	     * @brief Helper method to report the clustering summary.
279	     */
280	    void report_clustering_summary(ClusterLegalizer& cluster_legalizer,
281	                                   std::unordered_set<PackMoleculeId>& neighbor_pass_molecules,
282	                                   std::unordered_set<LegalizationClusterId>& orphan_window_clusters);
283	
284	    /**
285	     * @brief Helper method to create clusters with self, neighbor, and orphan window clustering.
286	     *
287	     *  @param cluster_legalizer The cluster legalizer which is used to create and grow clusters. Keeps track of
288	     *                           the clusters created and molecules clustered while checking cluster legality.
289	     *  @param p_placement       The partial placement used to guide where each molecule should be placed.
290	     */
291	    void create_clusters(ClusterLegalizer& cluster_legalizer,
292	                         const PartialPlacement& p_placement);
293	
294	    /**
295	     * @brief Helper method to perform initial placement on clusters created.
296	     *
297	     * Each cluster is placed by the initial placer at the grid location nearest
298	     * to the centroid of its atoms.
299	     */
300	    void place_clusters(const PartialPlacement& p_placement);
301	};
302	
303	/**
304	 * @brief The Naive Full Legalizer.
305	 *
306	 * This Full Legalizer will try to create clusters exactly where they want to
307	 * according to the Partial Placement. It will grow clusters from atoms that
308	 * are placed in the same tile, then it will try to place the cluster at that
309	 * location. If a location cannot be found, once all other clusters have tried
310	 * to be placed, it will try to find anywhere the cluster will fit and place it
311	 * there.
312	 */
313	class NaiveFullLegalizer : public FullLegalizer {
314	  public:
315	    using FullLegalizer::FullLegalizer;
316	
317	    /**
318	     * @brief Perform naive full legalization.
319	     */
320	    void legalize(const PartialPlacement& p_placement) final;
321	
322	  private:
323	    /**
324	     * @brief Helper method to create the clusters from the given partial
325	     *        placement.
326	     * TODO: Should return a ClusteredNetlist object, but need to wait until
327	     *       it is separated from load_cluster_constraints.
328	     */
329	    void create_clusters(const PartialPlacement& p_placement);
330	
331	    /**
332	     * @brief Helper method to place the clusters based on the given partial
333	     *        placement.
334	     */
335	    void place_clusters(const ClusteredNetlist& clb_nlist,
336	                        const PlaceMacros& place_macros,
337	                        const PartialPlacement& p_placement);
338	};
339	
340	/**
341	 * @brief APPack: A flat-placement-informed Packer Placer.
342	 *
343	 * The idea of APPack is to use the flat-placement information generated by the
344	 * AP Flow to guide the Packer and Placer to a better solution.
345	 *
346	 * In the Packer, the flat-placement can provide more context for the clusters
347	 * to pull in atoms that want to be near the other atoms in the cluster, and
348	 * repel atoms that are far apart. This can potentially make better clusters
349	 * than a Packer that does not know that information.
350	 *
351	 * In the Placer, the flat-placement can help decide where clusters of atoms
352	 * want to be placed. If this placement is then fed into a Simulated Annealing
353	 * based Detailed Placement step, this would enable it to converge on a better
354	 * answer faster.
355	 */
356	class APPack : public FullLegalizer {
357	  public:
358	    using FullLegalizer::FullLegalizer;
359	
360	    /**
361	     * @brief Run APPack.
362	     *
363	     * This will call the Packer and Placer using the options provided by the
364	     * user for these stages in VPR.
365	     */
366	    void legalize(const PartialPlacement& p_placement) final;
367	};

2	/**
3	 * @file
4	 * @author  Alex Singer
5	 * @date    February 2025
6	 * @brief   Defines the DetailedPlacer class which takes a fully legal clustering
7	 *          and placement and optimizes them while remaining legal.
8	 */
9	
10	#include <memory>
11	#include "ap_flow_enums.h"
12	#include "clustered_netlist_utils.h"
13	#include "placer.h"
14	#include "vpr_utils.h"
15	
16	/**
17	 * @brief The detailed placer in an AP flow.
18	 *
19	 * Given a fully legal clustering and clustered placement, will optimize the
20	 * solution while remaining fully legal (able to be used in the rest of the VPR
21	 * flow).
22	 */
23	class DetailedPlacer {
24	  public:
25	    virtual ~DetailedPlacer() {}
26	
27	    DetailedPlacer() = default;
28	
29	    /**
30	     * @brief Optimize the current legal placement.
31	     */
32	    virtual void optimize_placement() = 0;
33	};
34	
35	/**
36	 * @brief A factory method which creates a Detailed Placer of the given type.
37	 */
38	std::unique_ptr<DetailedPlacer> make_detailed_placer(e_ap_detailed_placer detailed_placer_type,
39	                                                     const BlkLocRegistry& curr_clustered_placement,
40	                                                     const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
41	                                                     const ClusteredNetlist& clustered_netlist,
42	                                                     t_vpr_setup& vpr_setup,
43	                                                     const t_arch& arch);
44	
45	/**
46	 * @brief The Identity Detailed Placer.
47	 *
48	 * This detailed placer does literally nothing to the legal placement. This
49	 * class is used as a placeholder to make the higher-level code easier to work
50	 * with.
51	 */
52	class IdentityDetailedPlacer : public DetailedPlacer {
53	  public:
54	    using DetailedPlacer::DetailedPlacer;
55	
56	    void optimize_placement() final {}
57	};
58	
59	/**
60	 * @brief The Annealer Detailed Placer.
61	 *
62	 * This Detailed Placer passes the legal solution into the Annealer in the
63	 * VPR flow (uses the legal solution as the initial placement). This performs
64	 * the Simulated Annealing algorithm on the solution at the cluster level to
65	 * try and find a better clustered placement.
66	 *
67	 * This Detailed Placer reuses the options from the Placer stage of VPR for this
68	 * stage. So options passed to the Placer will be used in here.
69	 */
70	class AnnealerDetailedPlacer : public DetailedPlacer {
71	  public:
72	    /**
73	     * @brief Construct the Annealer Detailed Placer class.
74	     *
75	     *  @param curr_clustered_placement
76	     *      The legalized placement solution to pass as the initial placement
77	     *      into the annealer.
78	     *  @param atom_netlist
79	     *      The netlist of atoms in the circuit.
80	     *  @param clustered_netlist
81	     *      The netlist of clusters created by the Full Legalizer.
82	     *  @param vpr_setup
83	     *      The setup variables, used to get the params from the user.
84	     *  @param arch
85	     *      The FPGA architecture to optimize onto.
86	     */
87	    AnnealerDetailedPlacer(const BlkLocRegistry& curr_clustered_placement,
88	                           const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
89	                           const ClusteredNetlist& clustered_netlist,
90	                           t_vpr_setup& vpr_setup,
91	                           const t_arch& arch);
92	
93	    /**
94	     * @brief Run the annealer.
95	     */
96	    void optimize_placement() final;
97	
98	  private:
99	    /// @brief The placer class, which contains the annealer.
100	    std::unique_ptr<Placer> placer_;
101	
102	    /// @brief A lookup between the block pin indices and pb graph pins.
103	    IntraLbPbPinLookup pb_gpin_lookup_;
104	
105	    /// @brief A lookup between CLB pins and atom pins.
106	    ClusteredPinAtomPinsLookup netlist_pin_lookup_;
107	};

1	#pragma once
2	/**
3	 * @file
4	 * @author  Alex Singer and Robert Luo
5	 * @date    October 2024
6	 * @brief   The declarations of the Analytical Solver base class which is used
7	 *          to define the functionality of all solvers used in the AP flow.
8	 */
9	
10	#include <memory>
11	#include <tuple>
12	#include "ap_flow_enums.h"
13	#include "ap_netlist.h"
14	#include "device_grid.h"
15	#include "physical_types.h"
16	#include "place_delay_model.h"
17	#include "vtr_strong_id.h"
18	#include "vtr_vector.h"
19	
20	#ifdef EIGEN_INSTALLED
21	// The eigen library contains a warning in GCC13 for a null dereference. This
22	// causes the CI build to fail due to the warning. Ignoring the warning for
23	// these include files. Using push to return to the state of GCC diagnostics.
24	#pragma GCC diagnostic push
25	#pragma GCC diagnostic ignored "-Wnull-dereference"
26	
27	#include "Eigen/Sparse"
28	
29	// Pop the GCC diagnostics state back to what it was before.
30	#pragma GCC diagnostic pop
31	#endif // EIGEN_INSTALLED
32	
33	// Forward declarations
34	struct PartialPlacement;
35	class APNetlist;
36	class AtomNetlist;
37	class PreClusterTimingManager;
38	
39	/**
40	 * @brief A strong ID for the rows in a matrix used during solving.
41	 *
42	 * This gives a linearized ID for each of the moveable blocks from 0 to the
43	 * number of moveable blocks.
44	 */
45	typedef vtr::StrongId<struct ap_row_id_tag, size_t> APRowId;
46	
47	/**
48	 * @brief The Analytical Solver base class
49	 *
50	 * This provides functionality that all Analytical Solvers will use.
51	 *
52	 * It provides a standard interface that all Analytical Solvers must implement
53	 * so they can be used interchangeably. This makes it very easy to test and
54	 * compare different solvers.
55	 */
56	class AnalyticalSolver {
57	  public:
58	    virtual ~AnalyticalSolver() {}
59	
60	    /**
61	     * @brief Constructor of the base AnalyticalSolver class
62	     *
63	     * Initializes the internal data members of the base class which are useful
64	     * for all solvers.
65	     */
66	    AnalyticalSolver(const APNetlist& netlist,
67	                     const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
68	                     const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
69	                     float ap_timing_tradeoff,
70	                     int log_verbosity);
71	
72	    /**
73	     * @brief Run an iteration of the solver using the given partial placement
74	     *        as a hint for what a "legal" solution would look like.
75	     *
76	     * Each solver is trying to optimize its own objective function. The solver
77	     * is also, at the same time, trying to approach a solution that is "similar"
78	     * to the hint provided. The larger the iteration, the more influenced the
79	     * solver is by the hint provided.
80	     *
81	     * It is implied that the hint partial placement is a placement which has
82	     * gone through some form of legalization. This allows the solver to
83	     * optimize its objective function, while also trying to approach a more
84	     * legal solution.
85	     *
86	     *  @param iteration    The current iteration number of the Global Placer.
87	     *  @param p_placement  A "hint" to a legal solution that the solver should
88	     *                      try and be like.
89	     */
90	    virtual void solve(unsigned iteration, PartialPlacement& p_placement) = 0;
91	
92	    /**
93	     * @brief Print statistics on the analytical solver.
94	     *
95	     * This is expected to be called after global placement to collect cumulative
96	     * information on how the solver performed.
97	     */
98	    virtual void print_statistics() = 0;
99	
100	    /**
101	     * @brief Update the net weights according to the criticality of the nets.
102	     *
103	     *  @param pre_cluster_timing_manager
104	     *      The timing manager which manages the criticalities of the nets.
105	     */
106	    virtual void update_net_weights(const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager) = 0;
107	
108	  protected:
109	    /// @brief The APNetlist the solver is optimizing over. It is implied that
110	    ///        the netlist is not being modified during global placement.
111	    const APNetlist& netlist_;
112	
113	    /// @brief The Atom netlist the solver is optimizing over. It is implied
114	    ///        that the atom netlist is not being modified during global
115	    ///        placement.
116	    const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist_;
117	
118	    /// @brief The number of moveable blocks in the netlist. This is helpful
119	    ///        when allocating matrices.
120	    size_t num_moveable_blocks_ = 0;
121	
122	    /// @brief The number of fixed blocks in the netlist.
123	    size_t num_fixed_blocks_ = 0;
124	
125	    /// @brief A lookup between a moveable APBlock and its linear ID from
126	    ///        [0, num_moveable_blocks). Fixed blocks will return an invalid row
127	    ///        ID. This is useful when knowing which row in the matrix
128	    ///        corresponds with the given APBlock.
129	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, APRowId> blk_id_to_row_id_;
130	
131	    /// @brief A reverse lookup between the linear moveable block ID and the
132	    ///        APBlock it represents. useful when getting the results from the
133	    ///        solver.
134	    vtr::vector<APRowId, APBlockId> row_id_to_blk_id_;
135	
136	    /// @brief The base weight of each net in the AP netlist. This weight can
137	    ///        be used to make the solver more interested in some nets over
138	    ///        others. These weights can be any positive value, but are often
139	    ///        between 0 and 1.
140	    vtr::vector<APNetId, float> net_weights_;
141	
142	    /// @brief A vector of blocks in the netlist which are not connected to any
143	    ///        non-ignored nets. Since the blocks have no connection to any
144	    ///        other blocks in the netlist, the are not considered movable or
145	    ///        fixed. The solver will just leave these blocks wherever they were
146	    ///        legalized.
147	    std::vector<APBlockId> disconnected_blocks_;
148	
149	    /// @brief The width of the device grid. Used for initializing the positions
150	    ///        of blocks in the grid.
151	    size_t device_grid_width_;
152	
153	    /// @brief The height of the device grid. Used for initializing the positions
154	    ///        of blocks in the grid.
155	    size_t device_grid_height_;
156	
157	    /// @brief The number of layers in the device grid. Used for initializing
158	    ///        the positions of blocks in the grid.
159	    size_t device_grid_num_layers_;
160	
161	    /// @brief The AP timing tradeoff term used during global placement. Decides
162	    ///        how much the solver cares about timing vs wirelength.
163	    float ap_timing_tradeoff_;
164	
165	    /// @brief The verbosity of log messages in the Analytical Solver.
166	    int log_verbosity_;
167	};
168	
169	/**
170	 * @brief A factory method which creates an Analytical Solver of the given type.
171	 */
172	std::unique_ptr<AnalyticalSolver> make_analytical_solver(e_ap_analytical_solver solver_type,
173	                                                         const APNetlist& netlist,
174	                                                         const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
175	                                                         const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
176	                                                         const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
177	                                                         std::shared_ptr<PlaceDelayModel> place_delay_model,
178	                                                         float ap_timing_tradeoff,
179	                                                         unsigned num_threads,
180	                                                         int log_verbosity);
181	
182	/**
183	 * @brief An analytical solver which does not solve anything. This solver acts
184	 *        like the identity matrix in a system of equations and just passes the
185	 *        previous solution (from the partial legalizer) along. This solver
186	 *        should only be used for testing.
187	 *
188	 * In the first iteration, the solver will initialize the positions of blocks
189	 * to the center of the device.
190	 */
191	class IdentityAnalyticalSolver : public AnalyticalSolver {
192	  public:
193	    IdentityAnalyticalSolver(const APNetlist& netlist,
194	                             const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
195	                             const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
196	                             float ap_timing_tradeoff,
197	                             int log_verbosity)
198	        : AnalyticalSolver(netlist,
199	                           atom_netlist,
200	                           device_grid,
201	                           ap_timing_tradeoff,
202	                           log_verbosity) {}
203	
204	    /**
205	     * @brief "Solve" for the positions of the blocks given the current iteration
206	     *        and the placement produced by the partial legalizer in the last
207	     *        iteration.
208	     *
209	     * In this case, if the iteration is non-zero, the placement will not be
210	     * modified. In the first iteration (iteration 0), the placement will be
211	     * initialized to the center of the device.
212	     */
213	    void solve(unsigned iteration, PartialPlacement& p_placement) final;
214	
215	    /**
216	     * @brief Net weights are not used by this solver, so this function goes unused.
217	     */
218	    void update_net_weights(const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager) final;
219	
220	    /**
221	     * @brief This solver does not do any real work, so it has no statistics to
222	     *        report. Thus this function goes unused.
223	     */
224	    void print_statistics() final;
225	};
226	
227	// The Eigen library is used to solve matrix equations in the following solvers.
228	// The solver cannot be built if Eigen is not installed.
229	#ifdef EIGEN_INSTALLED
230	
231	/**
232	 * @brief An Analytical Solver which tries to minimize the quadratic HPWL
233	 *        objective:
234	 *              SUM((xmax - xmin)^2 + (ymax - ymin)^2) over all nets.
235	 *
236	 * This is implemented using a hybrid approach, which uses both the Clique and
237	 * Star net models. Since circuits are hypernets, a single net may connect more
238	 * than just two blocks. The Clique model creates a clique connection between
239	 * all blocks in the net (each block connects to each other block exactly once).
240	 * The Star model creates a "fake". moveable star node which all blocks in the
241	 * net connect to (but the blocks in the net do not connect to each other).
242	 *
243	 * The Star net model creates an extra variable for the solver to solve; however
244	 * it allows the matrix to become more sparse (compared to the Clique net model).
245	 * This solver uses a net pin threshold, where if the the number of pins in a
246	 * net is larger than that threshold, that net will use the Star net model, and
247	 * if the number of pins is smaller the Clique model is used.
248	 *
249	 * This technique was proposed in FastPlace, where they proved that, if the
250	 * weights of the Star and Clique connections are set to certain values, they
251	 * will both achieve the same answer (minimizing the quadratic HPWL objective).
252	 *          https://doi.org/10.1109/TCAD.2005.846365
253	 */
254	class QPHybridSolver : public AnalyticalSolver {
255	  private:
256	    /// @brief The threshold for the number of pins a net will have to use the
257	    ///        Star or Clique net models. If the number of pins is larger
258	    ///        than this number, a star node will be created.
259	    /// This number will not change the solution, but may improve performance if
260	    /// tuned properly. If too low, then more star nodes will be created which
261	    /// adds more variables to the linear system; if too high, then more clique
262	    /// connections will be created, which will make the coefficient matrix less
263	    /// sparse.
264	    static constexpr size_t star_num_pins_threshold = 3;
265	
266	    // The following constants are used to configure the anchor weighting.
267	    // The weights of anchors grow exponentially each iteration by the following
268	    // function:
269	    //      anchor_w = anchor_weight_mult_ * e^(iter / anchor_weight_exp_fac_)
270	    // The numbers below were empircally found to work well.
271	
272	    /// @brief Multiplier for the anchorweight. The smaller this number is, the
273	    ///        weaker the anchors will be at the start.
274	    static constexpr double anchor_weight_mult_ = 0.001;
275	
276	    /// @brief Factor for controlling the growth of the exponential term in the
277	    ///        weight factor function. Larger numbers will cause the anchor
278	    ///        weights to grow slower.
279	    static constexpr double anchor_weight_exp_fac_ = 5.0;
280	
281	    /// @brief Due to the iterative nature of Conjugate Gradient method, the
282	    ///        solver may overstep 0 to give a slightly negative solution. This
283	    ///        is ok, and we can just clamp the position to 0. However, negative
284	    ///        values that are too large may be indicative of an issue in the
285	    ///        formulation. This value is how negative we tolerate the positions
286	    ///        to be.
287	    static constexpr double negative_soln_tolerance_ = 1e-9;
288	
289	    /**
290	     * @brief Initializes the linear system of Ax = b_x and Ay = b_y based on
291	     *        the APNetlist and the fixed APBlock locations.
292	     *
293	     * This is the "ideal" quadratic linear system where no anchor-points are
294	     * used. This system will be solved in the first iteration of the solver,
295	     * then used to generate the next systems.
296	     */
297	    void init_linear_system();
298	
299	    /**
300	     * @brief Initializes the guesses which will be used in the solver.
301	     *
302	     * The guesses will be used as starting points for the CG solver. The better
303	     * these guesses are, the faster the solver will converge.
304	     */
305	    void init_guesses(const DeviceGrid& device_grid);
306	
307	    /**
308	     * @brief Helper method to update the linear system with anchors to the
309	     *        current partial placement.
310	     *
311	     * For each moveable block (with row = i) in the netlist:
312	     *      A[i][i] = A[i][i] + coeff_pseudo_anchor;
313	     *      b[i] = b[i] + pos[block(i)] * coeff_pseudo_anchor;
314	     * Where coeff_pseudo_anchor grows with each iteration.
315	     *
316	     * This is basically a fast way of adding a connection between all moveable
317	     * blocks in the netlist and their target fixed placement location.
318	     *
319	     * See add_connection_to_system.
320	     *
321	     *  @param A_sparse_diff    The ceofficient matrix to update.
322	     *  @param b_x_diff         The x-dimension constant vector to update.
323	     *  @param b_y_diff         The y-dimension constant vector to update.
324	     *  @param p_placement      The location the moveable blocks should be
325	     *                          anchored to.
326	     *  @param num_moveable_blocks  The number of moveable blocks in the netlist.
327	     *  @param row_id_to_blk_id     Lookup for the row id from the APBlock Id.
328	     *  @param iteration        The current iteration of the Global Placer.
329	     */
330	    void update_linear_system_with_anchors(Eigen::SparseMatrix<double>& A_sparse_diff,
331	                                           Eigen::VectorXd& b_x_diff,
332	                                           Eigen::VectorXd& b_y_diff,
333	                                           PartialPlacement& p_placement,
334	                                           unsigned iteration);
335	
336	    /**
337	     * @brief Store the x and y solutions in Eigen's vectors into the partial
338	     *        placement object.
339	     */
340	    void store_solution_into_placement(const Eigen::VectorXd& x_soln,
341	                                       const Eigen::VectorXd& y_soln,
342	                                       PartialPlacement& p_placement);
343	
344	    // The following variables represent the linear system without any anchor
345	    // points. These are filled in the constructor and never modified.
346	    // When the anchor-points are taken into consideration, the diagonal of the
347	    // coefficient matrix and the elements of the constant vectors are updated
348	    // and used in the solver. These are stored to prevent re-computing each
349	    // iteration.
350	
351	    /// @brief The coefficient matrix for the un-anchored linear system
352	    /// This is expected to be sparse. This is shared between the x and y
353	    /// dimensions.
354	    Eigen::SparseMatrix<double> A_sparse;
355	    /// @brief The constant vector in the x dimension for the linear system.
356	    Eigen::VectorXd b_x;
357	    /// @brief The constant vector in the y dimension for the linear system.
358	    Eigen::VectorXd b_y;
359	    /// @brief The number of variables in the solver. This is the sum of the
360	    ///        number of moveable blocks in the netlist and the number of star
361	    ///        nodes that exist.
362	    size_t num_variables_ = 0;
363	
364	    /// @brief The current guess for the x positions of the blocks.
365	    Eigen::VectorXd guess_x;
366	    /// @brief The current guess for the y positions of the blocks.
367	    Eigen::VectorXd guess_y;
368	
369	    /// @brief The total number of CG iterations this solver has performed so far.
370	    unsigned total_num_cg_iters_ = 0;
371	
372	  public:
373	    /**
374	     * @brief Constructor of the QPHybridSolver
375	     *
376	     * Initializes internal data and constructs the initial linear system.
377	     */
378	    QPHybridSolver(const APNetlist& netlist,
379	                   const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
380	                   const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
381	                   const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
382	                   float ap_timing_tradeoff,
383	                   int log_verbosity)
384	        : AnalyticalSolver(netlist,
385	                           atom_netlist,
386	                           device_grid,
387	                           ap_timing_tradeoff,
388	                           log_verbosity) {
389	        // Update the net weights. These net weights are used when the linear
390	        // system is initialized.
391	        update_net_weights(pre_cluster_timing_manager);
392	
393	        // Initializing the linear system only depends on the netlist and fixed
394	        // block locations. Both are provided by the netlist, allowing this to
395	        // be initialized in the constructor.
396	        // TODO: Investigate re-initializing the linear system every so often
397	        //       given changes in the net weights / timing.
398	        init_linear_system();
399	
400	        // Initialize the guesses for the first iteration.
401	        init_guesses(device_grid);
402	    }
403	
404	    /**
405	     * @brief Perform an iteration of the QP solver, storing the result into
406	     *        the partial placement object passed in.
407	     *
408	     * In the first iteration (iteration = 0), the partial placement object will
409	     * not be used and the result would be the solution to the quadratic hpwl
410	     * objective.
411	     *
412	     * In the following iterations (iteration > 0), the partial placement object
413	     * will be used as anchor-points to guide the solver to a "more legal"
414	     * solution (assuming the partial placement contains a legal solution).
415	     * Higher iterations use stronger attraction forces between the moveable
416	     * blocks and their anchor-points.
417	     *
418	     * See the base class for more information.
419	     *
420	     *  @param iteration    The current iteration of the Global Placer.
421	     *  @param p_placement  A "guess" solution. The result will be written into
422	     *                      this object.
423	     */
424	    void solve(unsigned iteration, PartialPlacement& p_placement) final;
425	
426	    /**
427	     * @brief Update the net weights according to the criticality of the nets.
428	     *
429	     *  @param pre_cluster_timing_manager
430	     *      The timing manager which manages the criticalities of the nets.
431	     */
432	    void update_net_weights(const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager) final;
433	
434	    /**
435	     * @brief Print statistics of the solver.
436	     */
437	    void print_statistics() final;
438	};
439	
440	/**
441	 * @brief An Analytical Solver which tries to minimize the linear HPWL objective:
442	 *          SUM((xmax - xmin) + (ymax - ymin)) over all nets.
443	 *
444	 * This is implemented using the Bound2Bound method, which iteratively sets up a
445	 * linear system of equations (similar to the QP Hybrid approach above) which
446	 * solves a quadratic objective function. For a net model, each block connects
447	 * to the current bounding blocks in the given dimension and the weight of this
448	 * connection is inversly proportional to the distance of the block to the bound.
449	 * After minimizing this system, the bounds are likely to change; so the system
450	 * needs to be reconstructed and solved iteratively.
451	 *
452	 * This technique was proposed in Kraftwerk2, where they proved that the B2B Net
453	 * Model will, in theory, converge on the linear HPWL solution.
454	 *          https://doi.org/10.1109/TCAD.2008.925783
455	 */
456	class B2BSolver : public AnalyticalSolver {
457	  private:
458	    /**
459	     * @brief Enumeration for different initial placements that this class can
460	     *        perform in the first iteration.
461	     *
462	     * TODO: Investigate other initial placement techniques, the first iteration
463	     *       can be very expensive.
464	     */
465	    enum class e_initial_placement_type {
466	        LeastDense //< Randomly place blocks as a uniform grid over the device.
467	    };
468	
469	    /// @brief Which initial placement algorithm to use in the first iteration.
470	    ///        In the first iteration, we need some solution to initialize the
471	    ///        bounds. Some papers have found that setting it to a random
472	    ///        initial placement is the best approach.
473	    static constexpr e_initial_placement_type initial_placement_ty_ = e_initial_placement_type::LeastDense;
474	
475	    /// @brief Since the weights in the B2B model divide by the distance between
476	    ///        blocks and their bounds, that distance may get very very close to
477	    ///        0. This causes the weight matrix to become numerically unstable.
478	    ///        We can guard against this by clamping the distance to not be smaller
479	    ///        than some epsilon.
480	    ///        Decreasing this number may lead to more instability, but can yield
481	    ///        a higher quality solution.
482	    static constexpr double distance_epsilon_ = 0.01;
483	
484	    /// @brief The gap between the HPWL of the current solved solution in the
485	    ///        B2B loop and the previous solved solution that is considered to
486	    ///        be close-enough to be converged (as a fraction of the current
487	    ///        solved solution HPWL).
488	    /// Decreasing this number toward zero would cause the B2B solver to run
489	    /// more iterations to try and reduce the HPWL further.
490	    static constexpr double b2b_convergence_gap_fac_ = 0.0001;
491	
492	    /// @brief The number of times the B2B loop should "converge" before stopping
493	    ///        the loop. Due to numerical inaccuracies, it is possible for the
494	    ///        HPWL to bounce up and down as it converges. Increasing this number
495	    ///        will allow more bounces which may get better quality; however
496	    ///        more iterations will need to be run.
497	    static constexpr unsigned target_num_b2b_convergences_ = 1;
498	
499	    /// @brief Max number of bound update / solve iterations. Increasing this
500	    ///        number will yield better quality at the expense of runtime.
501	    static constexpr unsigned max_num_bound_updates_ = 24;
502	
503	    /// @brief Max number of iterations the Conjugate Gradient solver can perform.
504	    ///        Due to the weights getting very large in the early iterations of
505	    ///        Global Placement, the CG solver may take a very long time to
506	    ///        converge; but the solution quality will not change much. By
507	    ///        default the max iteration is set to 2 * num_moveable_blocks;
508	    ///        which causes the first iteration of B2B to become quadratic in the
509	    ///        number of moveable blocks if it cannot converge. Found through
510	    ///        experimentation that this can be clamped to a much smaller number
511	    ///        to prevent this behaviour and get good runtime.
512	    // TODO: Need to investigate this more to find a good number for this.
513	    // TODO: Should this be a proportion of the design size?
514	    static constexpr unsigned max_cg_iterations_ = 100;
515	
516	    // The following constants are used to configure the anchor weighting.
517	    // The weights of anchors grow exponentially each iteration by the following
518	    // function:
519	    //      anchor_w = anchor_weight_mult_ * e^(iter / anchor_weight_exp_fac_)
520	    // The numbers below were empircally found to work well.
521	
522	    /// @brief Multiplier for the anchorweight. The smaller this number is, the
523	    ///        weaker the anchors will be at the start.
524	    static constexpr double anchor_weight_mult_ = 0.01;
525	
526	    /// @brief Factor for controlling the growth of the exponential term in the
527	    ///        weight factor function. Larger numbers will cause the anchor
528	    ///        weights to grow slower.
529	    static constexpr double anchor_weight_exp_fac_ = 5.0;
530	
531	    /// @brief Factor for controlling the strength of the timing term in the
532	    ///        objective relative to the wirelength term. By increasing this
533	    ///        number, the solver will focus more on timing and less on wirelength.
534	    static constexpr double timing_slope_fac_ = 0.75;
535	
536	  public:
537	    B2BSolver(const APNetlist& ap_netlist,
538	              const DeviceGrid& device_grid,
539	              const AtomNetlist& atom_netlist,
540	              const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager,
541	              std::shared_ptr<PlaceDelayModel> place_delay_model,
542	              float ap_timing_tradeoff,
543	              int log_verbosity)
544	        : AnalyticalSolver(ap_netlist,
545	                           atom_netlist,
546	                           device_grid,
547	                           ap_timing_tradeoff,
548	                           log_verbosity)
549	        , pre_cluster_timing_manager_(pre_cluster_timing_manager)
550	        , place_delay_model_(place_delay_model) {}
551	
552	    /**
553	     * @brief Perform an iteration of the B2B solver, storing the result into
554	     *        the partial placement object passed in.
555	     *
556	     * In the first iteration (iteration = 0), the partial placement object will
557	     * be ignored, and a random initial placement will be used to initially
558	     * construct the system of equations. In all other iterations, the previous
559	     * solved solution will be used.
560	     *
561	     * The B2B solver will then iteratively solve the system of equations and
562	     * update the system to achieve a good HPWL solution which is close to the
563	     * linear HPWL solution. Due to numerical issues with this algorithm, we will
564	     * likely not converge on the true minimum HPWL solution, but it should be
565	     * close.
566	     *
567	     * See the base class for more information.
568	     *
569	     *  @param iteration
570	     *      The current iteration of the Global Placer
571	     *  @param p_placement
572	     *      A "guess" solution. The result will be written into this object.
573	     *      In all iterations other than the first, this solution will be used
574	     *      as anchor-points in the system.
575	     */
576	    void solve(unsigned iteration, PartialPlacement& p_placement) final;
577	
578	    /**
579	     * @brief Update the net weights. Currently unused by the B2B solver.
580	     *
581	     * TODO: Investigate weighting by some factor of fanout.
582	     */
583	    void update_net_weights(const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager);
584	
585	    /**
586	     * @brief Print overall statistics on this solver.
587	     *
588	     * This is expected to be called after all iterations of Global Placement
589	     * has been complete.
590	     */
591	    void print_statistics() final;
592	
593	  private:
594	    /**
595	     * @brief Run the B2B outer solving loop.
596	     *
597	     * The placement in p_placement should be initialized with the initial
598	     * positions of the blocks that the B2B algorithm should use to build the
599	     * first system of equations. This placement will be iteratively updated
600	     * with better and better solutions as B2B iterates.
601	     *
602	     * If iteration is 0, no anchor-blocks will be added to the system, otherwise
603	     * the solution in block_locs_legalized will be used as anchor-blocks.
604	     */
605	    void b2b_solve_loop(unsigned iteration, PartialPlacement& p_placement);
606	
607	    /**
608	     * @brief Randomly distributes AP blocks using a normal distribution.
609	     */
610	    void initialize_placement_random_normal(PartialPlacement& p_placement);
611	
612	    /**
613	     * @brief Randomly distributes AP blocks using a uniform distribution.
614	     */
615	    void initialize_placement_random_uniform(PartialPlacement& p_placement);
616	
617	    /**
618	     * @brief Randomly distributes AP blocks using as a uniform grid.
619	     */
620	    void initialize_placement_least_dense(PartialPlacement& p_placement);
621	
622	    /**
623	     * @brief Add a weighted connection to the linear system between the first
624	     *        and second blocks for a single dimension.
625	     *
626	     * This method is used to construct different linear systems for different
627	     * dimensions (x and y). Since the act of adding weighted connections is the
628	     * same regardless of dimension, this method passes in dimension-specific
629	     * information to be updated.
630	     *
631	     *  @param first_blk_id
632	     *  @param second_blk_id
633	     *  @param num_pins
634	     *      The number of pins in the hypernet connecting the two blocks.
635	     *  @param blk_locs
636	     *      The location of all blocks in a given dimension.
637	     *  @param triplet_list
638	     *      The triplet list which will be used to construct the connectivity
639	     *      matrix for this dimension.
640	     *  @param b
641	     *      The constant vector for this dimension.
642	     */
643	    void add_connection_to_system(APBlockId first_blk_id,
644	                                  APBlockId second_blk_id,
645	                                  size_t num_pins,
646	                                  double net_w,
647	                                  const vtr::vector<APBlockId, double>& blk_locs,
648	                                  std::vector<Eigen::Triplet<double>>& triplet_list,
649	                                  Eigen::VectorXd& b);
650	
651	    enum class CentralDifferenceDim {
652	        X,
653	        Y,
654	        Layer,
655	    };
656	
657	    /**
658	     * @brief Computes the central-difference approximation of d(delay)/d(position)
659	     *        for one spatial dimension.
660	     *
661	     * Queries the delay model one tile forward and one tile backward from the
662	     * sink along @p dim and averages the two finite differences. Out-of-bounds
663	     * neighbors and missing-path sentinel values are handled gracefully:
664	     * only valid neighbor delays contribute to the result.
665	     *
666	     *  @param driver_block_loc   Physical tile location of the driver block.
667	     *  @param sink_block_loc     Physical tile location of the sink block.
668	     *  @param current_edge_delay Delay from the driver to the sink at their
669	     *                            current locations.
670	     *  @param dim                Spatial dimension to differentiate along.
671	     *
672	     *  @return Approximate derivative of delay with respect to tile position in
673	     *          @p dim. Positive values indicate delay increases as the sink
674	     *          moves further from the driver.
675	     */
676	    double get_central_difference(const t_physical_tile_loc& driver_block_loc,
677	                                  const t_physical_tile_loc& sink_block_loc,
678	                                  double current_edge_delay,
679	                                  CentralDifferenceDim dim);
680	
681	    /**
682	     * @brief Get the instantaneous derivative of delay for the given driver
683	     *        and sink pair.
684	     *
685	     * The instantaneous derivative gives the amount delay would increase or
686	     * decrease for a change in distance by one unit. This is passed into the
687	     * objective function to help guide the solver to trading off timing and
688	     * wirelength.
689	     *
690	     * The delay derivative is computed on the legalized placement, not the
691	     * currently solved placement, so it is constant within a solver iteration.
692	     * This keeps the derivative stable: recomputing it from the solver's own
693	     * moving blocks may cause oscillation.
694	     *
695	     *  @param driver_blk
696	     *      The driver block for the edge to get the derivative of.
697	     *  @param sink_blk
698	     *      The sink block for the edge to get the derivative of.
699	     *
700	     *  @return The instantaneous derivative of delay with respect to distance
701	     *          in the x, y, and z dimensions respectively.
702	     */
703	    std::tuple<double, double, double> get_delay_derivative(APBlockId driver_blk,
704	                                                            APBlockId sink_blk);
705	
706	    /**
707	     * @brief Get normalization factors to normalize away time units out of the
708	     *        objective.
709	     *
710	     *  @param driver_blk
711	     *      The driver block of the edge to normalize the objecive for.
712	     */
713	    std::tuple<double, double, double> get_delay_normalization_facs(APBlockId driver_blk);
714	
715	    /**
716	     * @brief Initializes the linear system with the given partial placement.
717	     *
718	     * Blocks will be connected to the bounding blocks of their nets using
719	     * weighted connections, with weight inversly proportional to the distance
720	     * between blocks and the bounds. When solved in a quadratic equation this
721	     * approximates a linear equation.
722	     *
723	     * This will set the connectivity matrices (A) and constant vectors (b) to
724	     * be solved by B2B.
725	     */
726	    void init_linear_system(PartialPlacement& p_placement, unsigned iteration);
727	
728	    /**
729	     * @brief Updates the linear system with anchor-blocks from the legalized
730	     *        solution.
731	     */
732	    void update_linear_system_with_anchors(unsigned iteration);
733	
734	    /**
735	     * @brief Solves the linear system of equations using the connectivity
736	     *        matrix (A), the constant vector (b), and a guess for the solution.
737	     */
738	    Eigen::VectorXd solve_linear_system(Eigen::SparseMatrix<double>& A,
739	                                        Eigen::VectorXd& b,
740	                                        Eigen::VectorXd& guess);
741	
742	    /**
743	     * @brief Store the solutions from the linear system into the partial
744	     *        placement object for the given dimension.
745	     *
746	     * Note: The dim_soln may be modified if it is found that the solution is
747	     *       impossible (e.g. has negative positions).
748	     *
749	     *  @param dim_soln
750	     *      The solution of the linear system for a given dimension.
751	     *  @param block_dim_locs
752	     *      The block locations in the partial placement for the dimension.
753	     *  @param dim_max_pos
754	     *      The maximum position allowed for the dimension. For example, for the
755	     *      x-dimension, this would be the width of the device. This is used to
756	     *      ensure that the positions do not go off device.
757	     */
758	    void store_solution_into_placement(Eigen::VectorXd& dim_soln,
759	                                       vtr::vector<APBlockId, double>& block_dim_locs,
760	                                       double dim_max_pos);
761	
762	    /**
763	     * @brief Does the FPGA that the AP flow is currently targeting have more
764	     *        than one die. Having multiple dies would imply that the solver
765	     *        needs to add another dimension to solve for.
766	     */
767	    inline bool is_multi_die() const {
768	        return device_grid_num_layers_ > 1;
769	    }
770	
771	    // The following are variables used to store the system of equations to be
772	    // solved in the x and y dimensions. The equations are of the form:
773	    //          Ax = b
774	    // There are two sets of matrices and vectors since the x and y dimensions
775	    // of the objective are independent and can be solved separately.
776	    // These are updated each iteration of the B2B loop.
777	
778	    /// @brief The coefficient / connectivity matrix for the x dimension.
779	    Eigen::SparseMatrix<double> A_sparse_x;
780	    /// @brief The coefficient / connectivity matrix for the y dimension.
781	    Eigen::SparseMatrix<double> A_sparse_y;
782	    /// @brief The coefficient / connectivity matrix for the z dimension (layer dimension).
783	    Eigen::SparseMatrix<double> A_sparse_z;
784	    /// @brief The constant vector in the x dimension.
785	    Eigen::VectorXd b_x;
786	    /// @brief The constant vector in the y dimension.
787	    Eigen::VectorXd b_y;
788	    /// @brief The constant vector in the z dimension (layer dimension).
789	    Eigen::VectorXd b_z;
790	
791	    // The following is the solution of the previous iteration of this solver.
792	    // They are updated at the end of solve() and are used as the starting point
793	    // for the next call to solve.
794	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, double> block_x_locs_solved;
795	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, double> block_y_locs_solved;
796	    // NOTE: For speed, this vector is unused if a device has only one die.
797	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, double> block_z_locs_solved;
798	
799	    // The following are the legalized solution coming into the analytical solver
800	    // (other than the first iteration). These are stored to be used as anchor
801	    // blocks during the solver.
802	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, double> block_x_locs_legalized;
803	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, double> block_y_locs_legalized;
804	    // NOTE: For speed, this vector is unused if a device has only one die.
805	    vtr::vector<APBlockId, double> block_z_locs_legalized;
806	
807	    /// @brief The total number of CG iterations that this solver has performed
808	    ///        so far. This can be a useful metric for the amount of work the
809	    ///        solver performs.
810	    unsigned total_num_cg_iters_ = 0;
811	
812	    /// @brief The total time spent building the linear systems in the B2B solve
813	    ///        loop so far. This includes creating connections between blocks
814	    ///        in the connectivity matrix and constant vector as well as adding
815	    ///        anchor connections.
816	    float total_time_spent_building_linear_system_ = 0.0f;
817	
818	    /// @brief The total time spent solving the linear systems in the B2B solve
819	    ///        loop so far. This includes creating the CG solver object and
820	    ///        actually solving for a solution.
821	    float total_time_spent_solving_linear_system_ = 0.0f;
822	
823	    /// @brief Timing manager object used for calculating the criticality of
824	    ///        edges in the graph.
825	    const PreClusterTimingManager& pre_cluster_timing_manager_;
826	
827	    /// @brief The place delay model used for calculating the delay between
828	    ///        two tiles on the FPGA. Used for computing the timing terms.
829	    std::shared_ptr<PlaceDelayModel> place_delay_model_;
830	};
831	
832	#endif // EIGEN_INSTALLED
833

1	#pragma once
2	/**
3	 * @file
4	 * @author  Alex Singer
5	 * @date    September 2024
6	 * @brief   Declaration of the gen_ap_netlist_from_atoms function which uses the
7	 *          results of the prepacker to generate an APNetlist.
8	 */
9	
10	// Forward declarations
11	class APNetlist;
12	class AtomNetlist;
13	class Prepacker;
14	class RamMapper;
15	class UserPlaceConstraints;
16	
17	/**
18	 * @brief Use the results from prepacking the atom netlist to generate an APNetlist.
19	 *
20	 * RAM atoms that belong to the same PhysicalRamGroup (as determined by the
21	 * ram_mapper) are collapsed into a single AP block containing all the
22	 * group's molecules, so the global placer treats them as one moveable unit.
23	 *
